title: Mercedes F1 Wheel — Walkthrough
prompt: Walk me through this Mercedes-AMG F1 steering wheel.

sources.value: Carbon-fibre chassis, suede grips, magnesium quick-release

sources.title: Why this wheel is its own computer
sources.body: An F1 wheel is the driver's primary interface to a ~$15M race car. It carries the dash display, every in-race adjustment (brake bias, differential, fuel mix, ERS deployment, torque maps, DRS, pit limiter), the radio, and the clutch + shift mechanics. The driver makes 50-100 mid-lap inputs without taking their hands off the grips.

sources.title: Race-day adjustments
items: Brake bias — rotary, shifts F/R brake pressure split
Differential — rotary, locks/opens the diff for corner entry / mid / exit
Fuel mix — multifunction switch, lean ↔ rich
Torque map — multifunction, engine character per stint
ERS deployment — paddle / button, harvests + deploys battery
DRS — button or paddle, opens rear wing on straights
Pit limiter — button, caps speed in pit lane
Drink — button, releases water from helmet bladder
Radio — button, talks to the pit wall

sources.title: What this CAD does NOT model
items: Individual button surfaces (the real wheel has ~25 buttons; here they are decals on a flat plastic face)
LED rev strip across the top
Carbon-fibre weave detail (treated as plain matte black plastic for v0.1 visualisation)

sources.name: Mercedes F1 Steering Wheel
sources.finding: The centrepiece. Sits on the workbench so the driver-controls layout is readable from the user's seat. Bright translucent shader is the spatial-language signal that this is the subject of every other element in the scene.

sources.name: Left Grip Handle
sources.finding: Suede-wrapped handle the driver's left hand never leaves. Behind it: the left paddle (downshift) and the left clutch paddle (bite-point control on race starts).

sources.name: Right Grip Handle
sources.finding: Right-hand grip. Behind it: the right paddle (upshift) and the right clutch paddle. On many seasons the DRS button also lives on the back of this grip.

sources.name: Center Console / Display Bay
sources.finding: Houses the LCD dash readout (gear, speed, lap delta, tyre temps, fuel, ERS state). Surrounded by the most frequently-used buttons: radio, OK / confirm, drink, BB+ / BB-.

sources.name: Top-Left Control Bay
sources.finding: On most Mercedes wheels this carries the brake-bias rotary and the clutch-bite rotary. Brake bias is adjusted into and out of slow corners; bite-point is set on the formation lap.

sources.name: Top-Right Control Bay
sources.finding: Differential rotary + strategy multifunction. Diff entry / mid / exit settings get touched on every lap. Strategy mode swaps the whole map set: race / qualifying / safety car / cool-down.

sources.name: Mercedes Star — Quick-Release Nut
sources.finding: The team logo is the visible face of the quick-release collar. Pull, the wheel comes off in ~1 second — required by FIA regulation so a driver can egress in <5 seconds.

sources.name: Lower Rim Bar
sources.finding: The structural cross-piece that joins the left and right grip handles into one composite chassis. Typically holds the pit-limiter button + the burnout / start-mode trigger in a centre position.

sources.name: DRS Button
sources.finding: Drag Reduction System. A single press opens the rear-wing upper flap, stalling the wing and shedding ~15% of drag for ~9 m/s top-end on a long straight. Hold-to-arm on some wheels; close happens automatically on brake or lift.

sources.name: PC Button (Pit Confirm)
sources.finding: Pit confirm. Latches the pit-lane strategy preset the engineer just radio'd in (tyre compound, fuel plan, front-wing setting), so the driver enters the pit window with the right map already loaded.

sources.name: PL Button (Pit Limiter)
sources.finding: Pit limiter. Caps the car's throttle so it can't exceed the regulated pit-lane speed (80 km/h race / 60 km/h practice). Latches on at the entry line; the driver flicks it off at the exit.

sources.name: +1 Button
sources.finding: Generic +1 increment. Bumps whichever rotary is currently selected on the dash by one position without rotating the dial — used when the driver can't take a hand off the grip to twist.

sources.name: BB+ / BB- (Brake Bias) Rotary
sources.finding: Brake bias adjustment. Each click shifts the front/rear hydraulic pressure split by ~0.5%. Drivers wind bias rearward into slow corners (rotation), forward into fast corners (stability).

sources.name: Differential Rotary (DIFF)
sources.finding: Differential lock setting. Three sub-rotaries (ENTRY, MID, EXIT) control how locked the diff is at each corner phase. Looser entry = more rotation. Tighter exit = traction off the corner.

sources.name: Strategy Rotary (STRAT)
sources.finding: Whole-car mode swap. Each position is a pre-baked map for race / quali / safety-car / VSC / formation-lap / cool-down. Changes torque map, ERS deploy, fuel mix, brake-by-wire, all in one click.

sources.name: RS Button (Radio / Talk)
sources.finding: Press-to-talk radio. Opens the link to the pit wall; release closes it. Engineers radio anytime, but the driver only transmits while pressing this.

sources.title: Rotaries change car behaviour mid-corner
sources.finding: Every rotary affects the chassis dynamic. A mis-clicked differential setting on corner entry can rotate the car off line. Drivers train these positions by feel — that is why the labels are recessed into the chassis, not raised: the finger reads the click, not the eyes.

options.label: Focus on grips + paddles
options.detail: Walk the user through the left/right handles and the paddles immediately behind them.

options.label: Focus on top control bays
options.detail: Brake bias, differential, strategy, clutch bite — the rotary cluster region.

options.label: Focus on the dash
options.detail: What the LCD shows during a lap; the buttons around it that the driver hits without looking.

options.label: Focus on the quick-release
options.detail: Explain the 1-second wheel removal and FIA egress timing rule.
